era_context: Classical Greece in the early Classical period, immediately following the Persian Wars. Athens is consolidating its naval power and democratic institutions. Sparta remains the dominant land power in Greece. The Delian League, nominally a defensive alliance against Persia, is increasingly dominated by Athens. China is in the Spring and Autumn period; Egypt is under Achaemenid Persian rule. This is a pivotal year for Athenian political transformation.

events.title: Cimon ostracized from Athens; democratic faction gains power
events.description: Cimon, the conservative aristocratic leader and military hero of the Persian Wars, is ostracized from Athens through a popular vote. His ostracism reflects a power struggle between the conservative faction (favoring alliance with Sparta and limited democracy) and the radical democratic faction (favoring expansion of the Delian League and democratic reforms). Ephialtes, the democratic reformer, leads the opposition to Cimon. This ostracism marks a decisive shift in Athenian politics toward radical democracy and imperial expansion. Cimon will spend ten years in exile.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War

events.certainty_note: Cimon's ostracism in 461 BCE is well-attested in multiple ancient sources. The date is secure.
cross_references: Part of broader democratic transformation of Athens

events.title: Ephialtes assassinated; democratic reforms continue
events.description: Ephialtes, the leading democratic reformer who orchestrated Cimon's ostracism, is assassinated by political opponents. The murder is attributed to conservative aristocrats who oppose his radical democratic agenda. Despite his death, Ephialtes' reforms—particularly the curtailment of the Areopagus (the aristocratic council) and the expansion of democratic institutions—are carried forward by his political allies, especially Pericles. His assassination marks the intensity of political conflict in Athens but does not reverse the democratic tide.
events.certainty_note: Ephialtes' assassination is attested in ancient sources, but the exact date is uncertain. Most sources place it in 461 BCE, shortly after Cimon's ostracism, but some suggest 462 BCE. The identity of the assassins is not definitively known.

events.title: First Peloponnesian War begins; Athens and Sparta enter conflict
events.description: Tensions between Athens and Sparta escalate into open conflict. The immediate cause is disputed: some sources cite Athenian support for Megara (a Corinthian ally) against Corinth; others point to broader rivalry over hegemony in Greece. The conflict is characterized by raids, naval engagements, and proxy wars. Unlike the later Peloponnesian War (431–404 BCE), this conflict is intermittent and less devastating, but it marks the beginning of a 30-year period of Athenian-Spartan rivalry. The war reflects the incompatibility of Athenian naval imperialism and Spartan land-based hegemony.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1

events.certainty_note: The outbreak of the First Peloponnesian War in 461 BCE is well-attested in ancient sources. Thucydides provides a detailed account of the causes and early phases.
cross_references: Part of broader pattern leading to -431-001 (Second Peloponnesian War)

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of Ephialtes' assassination is disputed; some sources place it in 462 BCE rather than 461 BCE. The causes of the First Peloponnesian War are debated: Thucydides emphasizes Corinthian grievances, but modern scholars debate the role of Athenian imperial ambitions versus Spartan fear. The significance of the war itself is sometimes downplayed in modern scholarship, which views it as a prelude to the more famous Peloponnesian War (431–404 BCE) rather than as a major conflict in its own right.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 461 BCE is dominated by Athenian sources, particularly Thucydides and Plutarch, both writing decades or centuries after the events. Spartan and Corinthian perspectives are largely absent or filtered through Athenian accounts. The sources reflect Athenian political interests and may overstate Athenian agency. Archaeological evidence is limited for this year. The record is richest for Athens and Greece; sparse for other regions.

graph_edges.note: Cimon's ostracism intensifies political conflict, contributing to Ephialtes' assassination
Ephialtes' death clears the way for Pericles' political dominance
Cimon's ostracism and the outbreak of war reflect the same political realignment
First Peloponnesian War establishes pattern of Athenian-Spartan rivalry leading to the Second Peloponnesian War
